perturbation.original_text: This Intellectual Property Agreement shall be construed and interpreted according to the laws of the State of Georgia, applicable contracts to be wholly performed within the State of Georgia.
perturbation.changed_text: This Intellectual Property Agreement shall be construed and interpreted according to the laws of the State of Georgia, regardless of where the contract is performed.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies that Georgia law applies only if the contract is 'wholly performed within the State of Georgia.' The modified text removes this condition, stating Georgia law applies regardless of the place of performance. This creates a conflict because Georgia law might not be applicable if the contract is performed elsewhere, and forum selection clauses may be unenforceable if they violate state law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Georgia Code § 13-1-11 addresses the interpretation of contracts based on the location of performance. The modified text contradicts this by asserting that Georgia law applies irrespective of where the contract is performed. The Georgia law states, 'The validity, form, and effect of all writings shall be determined by the laws of the place where executed. When such writings are intended to have effect in this state, they must be executed in conformity with the laws of this state, except as provided in Code Section 11-1-301.'
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Georgia Code § 13-1-11 (2023) - Validity and enforcement of obligations to pay attorney's fees upon notes or other evidence of indebtedness :: 2023 Georgia Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Georgia Code 2023 Georgia Code (here) 2022 Georgia Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 CODE OF GEORGIA Title 13 - CONTRACTS (§§ 13-1-1 — 13-12-5) Chapter 1 - GENERAL PROVISIONS (§§ 13-1-1 — 13-1-16) Section 13-1-11 - Validity and enforcement of obligations to pay attorney's fees upon notes or other evidence of indebtedness Universal Citation: GA Code § 13-1-11 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next (a) Obligations to pay attorney's fees upon any note or other evidence of indebtedness, in addition to the rate of interest specified therein, shall be valid and enforceable and collectable as a part of such debt if such note or other evidence of indebtedness is collected by or through an attorney after maturity, subject to subsection (b) of this Code section and to the following provisions: (1) If such note or other evidence of indebtedness provides for attorney's fees in some specific percent of the principal and interest owing thereon, such provision and obligation shall be valid and enforceable up to but not in excess of 15 percent of the principal and interest owing on said note or other evidence of indebtedness; (2) If such note or other evidence of indebtedness provides for the payment of reasonable attorney's fees without specifying any specific percent, such provision shall be construed to mean 15 percent of the first $500.00 of principal and interest owing on such note or other evidence of indebtedness and 10 percent of the amount of principal and interest owing thereon in excess of $500.00; and (3) The holder of the note or other evidence of indebtedness or his or her
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the condition that Georgia law applies only if the contract is wholly performed within the State of Georgia. The Law Explanation states that Georgia Code § 13-1-11 addresses the interpretation of contracts based on the location of performance. While the provided snippet from Georgia Code § 13-1-11 pertains to attorney's fees and doesn't directly address the location of contract performance, the Perturbation Explanation highlights that applying Georgia law regardless of where the contract is performed creates a conflict. This is because Georgia law might not be applicable if the contract is performed elsewhere. Therefore, the modification introduces a potential contradiction by attempting to apply Georgia law irrespective of the place of performance, which could conflict with established legal principles regarding choice of law and the location of contract performance, even though the snippet is not directly on point.

perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, Seller and Verso Technologies, Inc., a Minnesota corporation ("Buyer"), have entered into that certain Asset Purchase Agreement, dated as of the date hereof (the "Asset Purchase Agreement"), pursuant to which Buyer has the right to acquire the Purchased Assets of Seller and its Subsidiaries, as more particularly described in the Asset Purchase Agreement (all capitalized words and terms used herein and not otherwise defined herein shall have the meanings ascribed to them in the Asset Purchase Agreement); and
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, Seller and Verso Technologies, Inc., a Minnesota corporation ("Buyer"), have entered into that certain Asset Purchase Agreement, dated as of the date hereof (the "Asset Purchase Agreement"), pursuant to which Buyer has the option, but not the right, to acquire the Purchased Assets of Seller and its Subsidiaries, as more particularly described in the Asset Purchase Agreement (all capitalized words and terms used herein and not otherwise defined herein shall have the meanings ascribed to them in the Asset Purchase Agreement); and
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'right' to 'option, but not the right' alters the fundamental nature of the Asset Purchase Agreement. A right is an entitlement, whereas an option is a privilege that may or may not be exercised. This creates ambiguity regarding the Buyer's obligation to acquire the assets.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-301 defines the general obligations of the seller and buyer in a contract for the sale of goods. By changing 'right' to 'option, but not the right', it introduces uncertainty about whether there's actually a 'sale' as contemplated by the UCC, since the buyer now has no defined obligation to purchase.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-301. General Obligations of Parties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-301. General Obligations of Parties. The obligation of the seller is to transfer and deliver and that of the buyer is to accept and pay in accordance with the contract . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The change from 'right' to 'option, but not the right' fundamentally alters the buyer's obligation. UCC § 2-301 states "the obligation of the seller is to transfer and deliver and that of the buyer is to accept and pay in accordance with the contract." By removing the 'right' of the buyer to acquire the asset, the buyer no longer has the obligation to accept and pay. This introduces uncertainty regarding whether the contract constitutes a 'sale' as intended by the UCC, making it a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Seller does hereby transfer, sell, assign, convey and deliver to Backhaul all right, title and interest in, to and under the Assigned Intellectual Property, including, without limitation, the Trademarks and Patents set forth on Schedules A and B hereof, respectively, and all goodwill of the Purchased Business associated therewith.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller does hereby allow Backhaul temporary use in, to and under the Assigned Intellectual Property, including, without limitation, the Trademarks and Patents set forth on Schedules A and B hereof, respectively, and all goodwill of the Purchased Business associated therewith.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'transfer, sell, assign, convey and deliver' to 'allow temporary use' completely alters the nature of the agreement. The original wording suggests a permanent transfer of ownership, while the modified text indicates a temporary license or permission to use the intellectual property. This change significantly affects the legal implications regarding ownership and control of the IP.
perturbation.law_explanation: 35 U.S. Code § 261 governs the ownership and assignment of patents. This section states that patents have the attributes of personal property and can be assigned. By changing the contract to indicate 'temporary use' rather than 'transfer, sell, assign...', it implies that the intellectual property is not being assigned as personal property which contradicts the nature of patent ownership and transfer as defined by federal law. It is not a sale, which this section applies to.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 35 U.S. Code § 261 - Ownership; assignment | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 261 - Ownership; assignment U.S. Code Notes prev | next Subject to the provisions of this title, patents shall have the attributes of personal property. The Patent and Trademark Office shall maintain a register of interests in patents and applications for patents and shall record any document related thereto upon request, and may require a fee therefor. Applications for patent, patents, or any interest therein, shall be assignable in law by an instrument in writing. The applicant, patentee, or his assigns or legal representatives may in like manner grant and convey an exclusive right under his application for patent, or patents, to the whole or any specified part of the United States. A certificate of acknowledgment under the hand and official seal of a person authorized to administer oaths within the United States, or, in a foreign country, of a diplomatic or consular officer of the United States or an officer authorized to administer oaths whose authority is proved by a certificate of a diplomatic or consular officer of the United States, or apostille of an official designated by a foreign country which, by treaty or convention, accords like effect to apostilles of designated officials in the United States, shall be prima facie evidence of the execution of an assignment, grant or conveyance of a patent or application for patent. An interest that constitutes an assignment, grant or conveyance shall be void as against any subsequent purchaser or mortgagee for a valuable consideration, without notice, unless it is recorded in the Patent and Trademark Office within three months from its date or prior to the date of such subsequent purchase or mortgage. (July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 810 ; Pub. L. 93–596, § 1 , Jan. 2, 1975 , 88 Stat. 1949 ; Pub. L. 97–247, § 14(b) , Aug. 27, 1982

Applications for patent, patents, or any interest therein, shall be assignable in law by an instrument in writin
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts 35 U.S. Code § 261. The original text indicated a transfer, sale, assignment, conveyance, and delivery of intellectual property, aligning with the concept of assigning patents as personal property as stated in the law snippets: 'patents shall have the attributes of personal property... Applications for patent, patents, or any interest therein, shall be assignable in law by an instrument in writing.' The changed text replaces this with 'allow temporary use,' which contradicts the notion of assignment and ownership as defined by the law, because it implies a temporary license rather than a permanent transfer of ownership.
